attributes.stem: A 56-year-old man comes in to see his general practitioner. He is angry with a gastric surgeon that the practitioner has recently referred him to. The patient had presented with a four month history of anorexia and weight loss, he was diagnosed with gastric cancer and referred to this surgeon where he was assessed and counselled for surgery and gastric resection. The attending surgeon is about to go overseas for six days and has reassured the patient that the operation could safely be deferred and performed once they return.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate advice to give the patient?
options: Refer the patient to another surgeon
Contact the hospital chief executive officer where the surgeon works
Refer the patient to the hospital complaints officer
Send the patient to the Emergency Department for admission
attributes.explanation:
The surgeon has not been negligent in his proposed management of the patient and appears to have explained to the patient a clear plan of action. In reality, a one week delay in surgery for his condition would be quite in keeping with standard management practice. It is likely that the patient mistook the surgeon's overseas trip as an indifference to the patient's illness. The best course of action for the GP is reassurance for the patient and reinforcing the advice of the surgeon.

The patient does not require admission to hospital unless he becomes acutely unstable, which is not described in the stem. Referring him to another surgeon would likely delay the surgery even longer as they would need to see him, consent him and plan for the surgery themselves. Contacting the hospital chief executive officer would not be required unless the general practitioner was concerned about negligent practice, which has not been demonstrated in this case. Patients should always be offered the option to submit a complaint to the hospital if they feel this is necessary, however only providing this advice in the consult with the GP may enforce the patient's opinion that his surgery has been unfairly delayed, so must be done with adequate counselling and reinforcing the surgeon's plan.

It is important to allow the patient to express their concerns in this case and provide validation about these concerns, as a diagnosis of cancer is extremely scary and stressful for patients. If they feel heard and understood it is easier to back up the advice from the surgeon and make the patient feel less anxious and more comfortable about the decisions that has been made.
learningPoints: Good communication and reassurance are fundamental to sound medical practice.
references: Janeway M, Wilson S, Sanchez SE, et al. Citizenship and Social Responsibility in Surgery: A Review. JAMA Surg. 2022; 157:532-39.
